title: String Concatenation vs Addition
explanation: '+' with a string → concatenation. '-' forces numeric coercion. true = 1 for math. [] coerces to '' (empty string).
mentalModel: '+' with string = concatenation. All other operators force numeric coercion

title: typeof null and undefined
explanation: typeof null = 'object' is a JS bug. null == undefined is true by spec. null === undefined is false (different types).
mentalModel: typeof null = 'object' is a language bug. null and undefined are loosely equal but strictly different

title: Unary + Operator and Truthiness
explanation: Unary + converts to number: +true = 1. ! converts to boolean and negates: 'Lydia' is truthy → !'Lydia' = false.
mentalModel: Unary + = convert to number. ! = convert to boolean and negate

title: Double Negation (!!) — Falsy Values
output:
false
false
true
false
false
true
true
explanation: !! converts to boolean. Falsy values → false. Truthy → true. Gotcha: [] and {} are TRUTHY.
mentalModel: Only 8 falsy values: null, undefined, 0, -0, 0n, NaN, '', false. Everything else is truthy

title: new Number() vs Primitive — == and ===
explanation: new Number(3) is an object wrapper. == coerces (same value → true). === checks type (number vs object → false).
mentalModel: new Number() creates an object, not a number. == coerces, === does not

title: Postfix vs Prefix Increment
explanation: Postfix: return first (0), then increment (→1). Prefix: increment first (1→2), then return (2).
mentalModel: Postfix: return first, increment after. Prefix: increment first, return after

title: Operator Precedence — Mixed Types
explanation: Left-to-right: 3+4=7 (math), then 7+'5'='75' (concat). Once a string appears, everything concatenates.
mentalModel: Left-to-right evaluation. Once a string appears, everything after becomes concatenation

title: Object Stringification in Addition
mentalModel: Objects become '[object Object]' when coerced to string

title: Object Reference Equality
code:
console.log({ age: 18 } === { age: 18 });
console.log({ age: 18 } == { age: 18 });
explanation: Objects are compared by REFERENCE, not value. These are two different objects in memory — different references → both false.
mentalModel: Primitives compare by value. Objects compare by reference (same memory address)

title: parseInt with Non-Numeric Characters
explanation: parseInt reads characters until it hits an invalid one. '*' is not a valid digit, so it stops and returns 7. Only the leading valid digits are parsed.
mentalModel: parseInt stops at the first non-numeric character. It doesn't evaluate the expression

title: typeof typeof — Nested typeof
explanation: typeof 1 = 'number' (a string). typeof 'number' = 'string'. typeof always returns a string, so typeof typeof anything = 'string'.
mentalModel: typeof always returns a STRING. So typeof typeof X is always 'string'

title: Logical Operators — Return Values (not Booleans)
explanation: || returns the first truthy value, or the last value if all falsy. && returns the first falsy value, or the last if all truthy. They DON'T return true/false — they return the actual value.
mentalModel: || returns first truthy (or last value). && returns first falsy (or last value). NOT booleans

title: [] is Truthy, but [] == false is true
explanation: [] is truthy → !![] = true → true == true. But [] == false: JS coerces both sides. [].toString() = '' → '' == false → 0 == 0 → true. !![] = true, !!false = false → true === false → false.
mentalModel: Loose equality has a multi-step coercion algorithm. Array is truthy but coerces to empty string

title: Number.isNaN vs isNaN — Key Difference
explanation: Number.isNaN checks if the value IS NaN (must be number type AND NaN). isNaN first coerces to number then checks — 'Lydia' becomes NaN after coercion, so isNaN returns true.
mentalModel: Number.isNaN: is this value literally NaN? isNaN: does this become NaN when coerced to number?

title: !typeof — Negating a String
explanation: typeof randomValue = 'number' (a truthy string). !('number') = false. false === 'string' = false. The ! binds to typeof result first, not to the comparison.
mentalModel: !typeof X is always false because typeof returns a truthy string, and !truthyString = false
